1: Introductory temporal clause (wayhî bāyyāmîm hāhēm, "in those days") links this episode to the Philistine mobilization context. Achish's command to David — yādōaʿ tēdaʿ (literally "knowing you will know," infinitive absolute + imperfect = emphatic certainty) — underscores that this is not an invitation but a binding military commitment. The irony that David is now obligated to fight against Israel will drive 1 Samuel 28–29.
2: ʾattāh tēdaʿ ʾēt ʾăšer yaʿăśeh ʿabdəḵā — "you will see what your servant can do." David's reply is deliberately ambiguous — it commits to nothing specific. Achish interprets it as complete loyalty and names him permanent bodyguard (šōmēr lərōʾšî, keeper of my head). David never fights the battle; the Providence that resolves his dilemma comes from the Philistine commanders in 1 Samuel 29.
3: ʾôḇ (H178) and yiddəʿōnî (H3049) — "mediums and necromancers." ʾÔḇ (singular and collective) denotes the hollow pit or wineskin through which spirits were thought to speak, and by extension the practitioner. Yiddəʿōnî derives from yādaʿ (to know) — the "knowing one," a spirit-familiar. Both are banned by the Mosaic code (Leviticus 19:31; 1 Samuel 20:6, 27; Deuteronomy 18:11). Saul himself expelled (hiʿbîr, caused to pass/removed) them — and will now secretly seek one. The narrative plants the irony: the law Saul enforced against others he will break himself.
4: Geographical orientation: Shunem (Philistines, north side of Jezreel Valley) vs. Gilboa (Israelites, south side). The valley between them becomes the killing ground of 1 Samuel 31. The same Gilboa site will prompt David's lament in 2 Samuel 1:21 ("mountains of Gilboa, let there be no dew or rain on you").
5: wayyarʾ šāʾûl ʾet maḥănēh pəlištîm wayyirāʾ wayyeḥerad libbô məʾōd — "he was terrified; his heart shook with dread." Three fear-words: yārēʾ (to be afraid), ḥārad (to tremble/shudder), and məʾōd (greatly). The phrase wayyeḥerad libbô (his heart shook) uses lēḇ again as the seat of inner response. Saul's fear is extreme and total — the contrast with David, who "strengthened himself in YHWH" (1 Samuel 30:6) after an equally devastating situation, could not be sharper.
6: Three channels of revelation — all closed. Wayyišʾal šāʾûl baYHWH wəlōʾ ʿānāhû — "Saul inquired of the LORD, but the LORD did not answer him." The three failed channels: (1) baḥălōmôt — dreams; (2) baûrîm — the Urim (part of the priestly ephod, the sacred lot); (3) bannəḇîʾîm — prophets. This triad exhausts the legitimate means of divine revelation (cf. Numbers 12:6-8). The contrast with 1 Samuel 30:7-8 — where David inquires through the ephod and immediately receives a clear answer — marks the theological divide: YHWH answers the king he has chosen and is silent to the king he has rejected.
7: baʿălat ʾôḇ — "a woman who is a medium" (literally "mistress of a spirit-pit"). The feminine participle baʿălat (owner/possessor, from baʿal) used for the woman marks her as the authorized practitioner of her craft. Endor is in Manassite territory north of Jezreel — Saul must pass through or near Philistine-controlled terrain to reach her, adding physical danger to moral compromise.
8: wayyiṯḥappeś šāʾûl — "Saul disguised himself" (Hithpael of ḥāpas, H2664, to search out / conceal oneself). The Hithpael reflexive indicates active self-concealment. The disguise is both literal (changed clothes, night travel) and symbolic: Saul who presented himself in his full royal height is now hidden. Bāʾ baʿădê hallaylāh — "came in the cover of night" — darkness accompanies the illicit act.
9: The woman invokes Saul's own policy against her: ʾattāh yādaʿtā ʾēt ʾăšer ʿāśāh šāʾûl — "you know what Saul has done." She uses the familiar "Saul" as if speaking to an outsider, not knowing she is addressing Saul himself. The dramatic irony peaks here: Saul hears his own name and his own enforcement of the ban described to his face.
10: wayyiššāḇaʿ lāh šāʾûl baYHWH lēʾmōr — "Saul swore to her by the LORD." The formula ḥay YHWH (as the LORD lives) is the standard covenant oath. Saul invokes YHWH to protect a woman engaged in a practice YHWH forbids — the theological incoherence is complete. The oath itself is a further breach of the covenant he is already violating.
11: The woman asks mî ʾaʿăleh lāḵ — "who shall I bring up for you?" (ʿālāh, to go up — here causative: to cause to come up). The verb is technically used of ascending from Sheol — the underworld conceptualized as below ground (cf. Psalms 30:3; 1 Samuel 86:13). Whether the woman actually raised Samuel or performed a deception the text does not explicitly adjudicate; the narrative presents what follows as a genuine exchange.
12: wattiṣʿaq bəqôl gādôl — "she cried out with a loud voice." The sudden, surprised cry suggests the woman herself was startled — possibly indicating she expected her usual process of deception and received something unprecedented. Her immediate recognition of Saul (lāmmāh rimmîtānî wəʾattāh šāʾûl, "why did you deceive me? You are Saul!") occurs because seeing the genuine apparition revealed to her the identity of her client.
13: ʾĕlōhîm ʿōlîm min hāʾāreṣ — "I see a divine figure rising up from the earth." ʾĔlōhîm (H430) is grammatically plural but takes a plural participle (ʿōlîm, "rising"). The term can mean "gods," "divine beings," "angels," or (rarely) "the God." Here the plural form is conventionally explained as a plural of majesty describing a single figure. The ambiguity is significant: the text does not identify this figure as definitively "Samuel's spirit" — the narrator uses the woman's description, leaving the nature of the apparition theologically open while making Samuel's identity the working assumption of the narrative.
14: mah tāʾrô — "what does he look like?" (tāʾrô from tōʾar, H8389, form/appearance). The description — old man, wrapped in a robe (məʿîl) — triggers Saul's recognition: wayyēdaʿ šāʾûl kî šəmûʾēl hûʾ (Saul knew it was Samuel). The məʿîl is the priestly robe Samuel wore — the same garment whose edge Saul tore in 1 Samuel 15:27. Recognition through a distinctive garment connects this scene to the covenant rupture of 1 Samuel 15.
15: lāmmāh hirgaztanî — "why have you disturbed me?" (rāgaz, H7264, to quake/agitate). Samuel's first word is protest: the séance is a violation even of the dead's rest. Bəṣar lî məʾōd — "I am in desperate straits" — the same root ṣārar (to be cramped/distressed) used for extreme crisis. Saul then describes the triple silence of v6. Samuel's response to being called up is not sympathy but the reiteration of the verdict already pronounced.
16: sûr mēʿimmāḵ wayhî ʿārêḵā — "the LORD has turned away (sûr, H5493) from you and become your adversary (ṣar, H6145)." Sûr is the departure-verb also used in 16:14 for the departure of the rûaḥ. The noun ṣar (foe/adversary) shares its root with śāṭān in function — the accuser who stands against. YHWH, who was the divine warrior fighting for Israel, is now positioned as Saul's enemy. Saul's question "why do you ask me?" (lāmmāh tiš'al bî) receives the answer: there is no point asking the mediator when the one who would answer has become your opponent.
17: wayyaʿaś YHWH lô kaʾăšer dibbēr bəyādî — "the LORD has done to you exactly what he said through me." The formula kaʾăšer dibbēr bəyādî (as he spoke by my hand) identifies Samuel as the instrument of YHWH's word — prophetic fulfillment terminology. The verb qāraʿ (torn) echoes 1 Samuel 15:28; the same event is replayed in summary form: the kingdom was torn from Saul's hand and given to David (rēʿeḵā, "your neighbor," same word as 1 Samuel 15:28).
18: lōʾ šāmaʿtā bəqôl YHWH — "you did not obey the voice of YHWH." The šāmaʿ vocabulary from 1 Samuel 15 reappears as the explicit ground of the judgment. The failure was not listening-and-acting on Amalek (lōʾ ʿāśîtā ḥărôn ʾappô bāʿămālēq) — the fierce judgment not executed. The word ḥărôn (burning anger, H2740) marks the intensity of the divine command that was not carried out.
19: māḥār ʾattāh ûḇānêḵā ʿimmî — "tomorrow you and your sons will be with me." The adverb māḥār (tomorrow) is a timed death sentence. The phrase ʿimmî (with me) means "in Sheol/in the realm of the dead" — Samuel is speaking from there. The double transfer announced: Israel and Saul into Philistine hands, and Saul-and-sons to Samuel in death. Both parts are fulfilled in 1 Samuel 31.
20: wayyimahēr wayyipôl mᵉlōʾ qômātô ʾarṣāh — "Saul immediately fell full length on the ground." Mᵉlōʾ qômātô (literally "the fullness of his height") — Saul the tall man who stood head-and-shoulders above all Israel (1 Samuel 9:2) is now measured out on the ground. The narrative detail is an acted emblem: his physical height had been his royal credential; now he lies that same height on the floor.
21: The woman shifts from medium to host. wattarʾ ʾet šāʾûl kî nibhal məʾōd — "she saw how shaken (nāḇhal, H926, to be dismayed/confused) he was." Her concern is practical — he needs strength to make the journey back. The moral texture is complex: the woman who performed the forbidden séance acts with genuine compassion; the narrative does not moralize.
22: šimʿā nāʾ gam ʾattāh bəqôl šipḥāteḵā — "please listen to me" (šāmaʿ bəqôl — the obedience-of-hearing formula). The woman uses the vocabulary of covenantal hearing for a mundane request to eat. The phrase is ironic: the chapter turns on whether Saul listened to the right voices; now a necromancer uses the same idiom to urge physical sustenance.
23: waymāʾēn wayyōʾmer lōʾ ʾōḵal — "he refused, saying I will not eat." Māʾēn (H3985, to refuse/decline) — the same root used in 1 Samuel 15 for covenant refusal themes. Saul's initial refusal gives way under sustained pressure (wayyipṣar bô ʿăḇādāyw wəgam hāʾiššāh). The eating of the meal is a final act of hospitality; after it, Saul goes to his death.
24: The woman's meal: fattened calf (ʿēgel marbēq), flour kneaded, unleavened bread (maṣṣôt). Maṣṣôt is the bread of the Passover and sacred meals; its use here is probably not symbolic but reflects the fastest bread to prepare (no waiting for leavening). The hospitality given to the doomed king at the hands of one who practices what Saul outlawed is one of the story's final ironies.
25: wayyāqumû wayyēlǝḵû ballaylāh hahûʾ — "they rose and left that same night." The departure in darkness echoes the arrival in darkness (v8). Saul leaves Endor as he arrived — hidden, broken, and moving toward a death already announced. The chapter closes without commentary; the reader carries the weight of what was said in the darkness.
